title: Osteomalacia & rickets
body_html:
Osteomalacia & rickets

Osteomalacia is a metabolic bone disease characterised by incomplete mineralisation of mature organic bone matrix following growth plate closure.

Vitamin D deficiency is the most common cause and this may be linked to a history of limited sunlight exposure. Inadequate phosphate or calcium levels may also lead to osteomalacia and these may be secondary to malnutrition, malabsorption or impaired resorption or excretion (e.g. renal failure).

Rickets may be thought of as osteomalacia in children. Rickets is a metabolic bone disease characterised by defective mineralisation of the epiphyseal growth plate cartilage in children, resulting in skeletal deformities and growth retardation. Rickets and osteomalacia are different manifestations of the same underlying pathological process.

Osteomalacia vs osteoporosis
In osteomalacia the bone mass is normal, but the matrix to mineral ratio is increased, meaning there is less mineral in bone

In osteopenia and osteoporosis bone mass is decreased but the matrix to mineral ratio is not altered from normal bone.

Clinical features

OsteomalaciaRickets
Bone pain
Proximal muscle weakness
Spinal tenderness to percussion
Pseudofractures (Looser's zones)
Skeletal deformities
  • Short stature
  • protrusio acetabuli
Waddling gait		Bowed limbs (especially tibia)
Poor growth
Bone pain
Greenstick fractures
Skeletal abnormalities
  • Craniotabes
  • Skull embossing
  • Knock knees
  • Kyphoscoliosis or lumbar lordosis
  • Pigeon chest

Investigations
  • Calcium, phosphate, vitamin D and PTH levels
  • Renal function
  • Alkaline phosphatase
  • 24 hour urinary calcium
  • X-rays and DXA scan may be useful

ALP is high in the majority of osteomalacia patients except in those patients with hypophosphatasia

PTH is high in osteomalacia caused by vitamin D deficiency, renal failure, and hypocalcaemia.

Management
  • Vitamin D and calcium supplementation are the mainstay in most cases
  • Identification and treatment of any underlying cause.
